name: HWASan — Hardware-Assisted AddressSanitizer
summary: Compiler-instrumented MTE-style tagging using AArch64 Top-Byte-Ignore (8-bit tag in pointer upper byte) with software-managed shadow tag memory — works without MTE hardware. Software-tagged predecessor and design analogue of MTE. Smaller shadow than ASan (1/16 vs 1/8 memory). Default for Android system-image fuzzing pipeline.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Hardware-Assisted AddressSanitizer** is compiler-instrumented MTE-style tagging using AArch64 Top-Byte-Ignore (8-bit tag in pointer upper byte) with software-managed shadow tag memory — works without MTE hardware. It is the software-tagged predecessor and design analogue of MTE rather than a silicon-enforced mechanism.

Key properties:
- Smaller shadow than ASan (1/16 vs 1/8 memory)
- Per-allocation tagged pointers
- Stack-tagging IR pass
- Outlined check sequences with custom calling convention to keep code small
- Higher cost than MTE (everything is software) but lower than ASan

sections.title: Deployment and toolchain status
sections.content:
Ships in Clang/LLVM upstream; default for Android system-image fuzzing pipeline; precursor to MTE deployments. Widely used in Android system builds for fuzzing and debug.

The full ASan/MSan/TSan/UBSan family — of which HWASan is the AArch64-specialised tagging variant — is documented in the broader sanitizer survey under shadow-memory diagnostics.
